of the Invention This invention generally relates to wireless communication antennas and, more particularly, to a dual-band antenna impedance matching system and method thaT supplies dual-band impedance matches for an antenna. 2. Description of the Related Art The size of portable wireless communications devices, such as telephones, continues to shrink, even as more functionality is added. As a result, the designers must increase the performance of components or device subsystems and reduce their size, while packaging these components in inconvenient locations. One such critical component is the wireless communications antenna. This antenna may be connected to a telephone transceiver, for example, or a global positioning system (GPS) receiver. State-of-the-art wireless telephones are expected to operate in a number of different communication bands. In the US, the cellular band (AMPs), at around 850 megahertz (MHz), and the PC2176 MHz; Personal Communication System(PCS)at approximately l9lO MZ: Global Positioning Systems{GPS}al I .S GHz:. The wireless telephone antenna is conventionally contained within a device housing, either on an interior surface or in one of several locations under its skin. For example (he cellular and GPS antennas may be positioned underneath the plastic posterior casing that houses their electronic engine board(s). Many conventional devices rely oil near-field uir antennas to receive radio signal transmissions from satellites: other wireless telephones; short range Blue tooth® enabled systems, etc.. One such antenna is an inverted F. antenna (IFA), which relies on coupling with a ground plane whose length and shape can be manipulated for multiband or broadband tuning purposes {e . g., 824 to l9l0 MHz). Unfortunately because of their limited size; state-of the art IFAs are unable lo achieve good electrical performance in both these communication bands. Therefore, they provide either inadequate signal reception resulting from an impedance mismatch at one end ol (he spectrum: or adequate {even if suboptimal} receptivity al a middle point between cellular and GPS frequencies where their impedances match those of devices to which the antennas are coupled . To overcome this problem designers have replaced

ction into two parts,and inserting a screenshot capture in between as in the code below: new TouchAction(driver) .press(x,y) .moveTo(newX,newY) .perform(); takeScreenshot(); // My own implementation for readability new TouchAction(driver) .release() .perform(); I get thefollowing error: org.openqa.selenium.WebDriverException: ERROR running The issue you're encountering is likely due to releasing your touch before taking a screenshot. In Android, when performing drag-and-drop operations using Appium's `TouchActions`, it simulates continuouspressure andmovement on an element until explicitly released. To capture the screen while still holding down (during dragging), one approach would be: 1) Start pressing at initial coordinates. 2a- Simulate moving to a new position but do not release yet. 3b- Take screenshot immediately after or during this press-move action, before releasing it completely: 4c)- Then finally perform the full touchAction that includes `.release()`. However since Appium does allowsuch granular control directly out-of-the-box for capturing screens mid-drag operation without fully completing an element release; you may need to use a work-around or consider alternative strategies: ### Alternative Strategy (Recommended): 1. Use JavaScript Executor: Execute some custom JS code within the app'scontext that forces taking screenshots programmatically during certainstates of your test execution flow. 2a- Another option would be capturing UI Automator dump and parsing it for visual representation whichmight indirectly provide you with similar data as a screenshot but requires more effort on processing end. ```java // Example Java Code (Pseudocode) WebElement element = driver.findElement(By.id("your_element_id")); new TouchAction(driver).press(element, Duration.ofMillis(10)) .moveTo(new Coordinates(), duration) // Simulate the draghere with a delay. .perform(); ((JavascriptExecutor)(driver)).executeScript( "mobile: shell", Collections.singletonMap(KeyEvent.KEYCODE_CAMERA,"screencap /sdcard/screenshot.png")); // Then pull this file back from device or read it di
